title: Why taking notes?
html:
It is easy to fall into collecting notes as if the collection itself were the point — clipping, saving, filing away, and feeling productive for it. But a pile of notes you never return to is just a tidier kind of clutter. So don’t hoard notes just for the sake of it.

Notes earn their keep for a simpler, almost humbling reason. A note system is helpful because it can deal with, or at least mitigate, the forgetfulness nature of our brain. Our minds are wonderful at thinking and terrible at storing; what felt vivid and obvious last month quietly dissolves. A good note is a small defense against that erosion.

Seen this way, the practice does two things:

  • It lets you program your future self — leaving instructions, context, and hard-won conclusions where the person you’ll become can actually find them.
  • It is a way to revoke and unload the past self into the current and future self, carrying forward what you once understood instead of rediscovering it from scratch.
    • And here the craft matters: the quality and expressiveness of the note system affect how much information is lost during these unloadings. A rushed, cryptic line loses more of your past self than a clear, well-shaped one. Think of a note you wrote in a hurry years ago that now means nothing to you — that gap is exactly the loss worth designing against.

(A small idea I’ve been turning over after reading a friend’s garden of thoughts.)

  • How I Write Notes
